title: `transitive_closure()` drops self-loops
author: apsoras
body:
Summary: `transitive_closure()` drops self-loop edges present in the input graph.

If `a -> a` exists in the input graph, `a -> a` should appear in the output of `transitive_closure()`, but it does not, regardless of that vertex's connectivity (isolated or otherwise). 

Closures should probably not drop edges, even if they are of length 0.

The self loops do correctly appear in the input graph via `graph_from_data_frame()`, but not in the `transitive_closure()`.

side note: `transitive_closure()` also drops vertex names in the output which would be nice to fix as well

**sample data**
```R
sample <- dplyr::tribble(
    ~from_id, ~to_id,
    "a", "b",
    "b", "d",
    "c", "d", 
    "d", "e",
    "e", "e", # loop, inlet only
    "a", "a", # loop, outlet only
    "b", "b", # loop, inlet & outlet
    "f", "f", # loop, isolated
)
```

**expected output**
```
a -> a # loop
a -> b
a -> d
a -> e
b -> b # loop
b -> d
b -> e
c -> d
c -> e
d -> e
e -> e # loop
f -> f # loop
```

**operation**
```R
sample |> 
    igraph::graph_from_data_frame() |> 
    igraph::transitive_closure() |> 
    igraph::set_vertex_attr("name", value = igraph::vertex_attr(igraph::graph_from_data_frame(sample), "name")) |> 
    igraph::as_data_frame()
```

**actual output**
```
  from to
1    a  b
2    a  d
3    a  e
4    b  d
5    b  e
6    c  d
7    c  e
8    d  e
```
So we are missing all of the self-loops in the output. 

```
> sessionInfo()
R version 4.6.0 (2026-04-24 ucrt)
Platform: x86_64-w64-mingw32/x64
Running under: Windows 11 x64 (build 26100)

Matrix products: default
  LAPACK version 3.12.1

locale:
[1] LC_COLLATE=English_United States.utf8  LC_CTYPE=English_United States.utf8   
[3] LC_MONETARY=English_United States.utf8 LC_NUMERIC=C                          
[5] LC_TIME=English_United States.utf8    

time zone: America/New_York
tzcode source: internal

attached base packages:
[1] stats     graphics  grDevices utils     datasets  methods   base     

loaded via a namespace (and not attached):
 [1] tidyselect_1.2.1 compiler_4.6.0   magrittr_2.0.5   R6_2.6.1         generics_0.1.4   cli_3.6.6       
 [7] tools_4.6.0      igraph_2.3.1     pillar_1.11.1    glue_1.8.1       dplyr_1.2.1      tibble_3.3.1    
[13] utf8_1.2.6       vctrs_0.7.3      lifecycle_1.0.5  pkgconfig_2.0.3  rlang_1.2.0     
>

title: simple_cycles() returns empty for a graph with cycles
author: KevCaz
body:
Dear maintainer, 

While working with igraph I found what I believe is a bug. Hopefully I did not miss something obvious here. This may be a bug for the C package, but I have no experience with it.

Thanks for all your work on this. 

### What happens, and what did you expect instead?

Here is the issue `igraph::simple_cycles()` returns an empty list on a directed graph that **does** contain a cycle (a 2-cycle), while `is_acyclic()` correctly reports the graph is not acyclic. The bug is **vertex-order-dependent**: permuting the vertex sequence makes `simple_cycles()` find the cycle.


### To reproduce

```R
library(igraph)
#> 
#> Attaching package: 'igraph'
#> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
#> 
#>     decompose, spectrum
#> The following object is masked from 'package:base':
#> 
#>     union
m <- matrix(0, 6, 6, dimnames = list(as.character(1:6), as.character(1:6)))
m["1","2"] <- 1; m["1","6"] <- 1; m["2","6"] <- 1
m["3","6"] <- 1; m["5","4"] <- 1; m["5","6"] <- 1
m["6","3"] <- 1; m["6","4"] <- 1
g <- graph_from_adjacency_matrix(m, mode = "directed", weighted = TRUE)

is_acyclic(g) 
#> [1] FALSE
# no cycles
simple_cycles(g)
#> $vertices
#> list()
#> 
#> $edges
#> list()

# Permutation
perm <- match(igraph::V(g)$name, c("1","2","6","3","5","4"))
g2 <- igraph::permute(g, perm)
igraph::V(g2)$name         
#> [1] "1" "2" "6" "3" "5" "4"
igraph::simple_cycles(g2) 
#> $vertices
#> $vertices[[1]]
#> + 2/6 vertices, named, from f460b4d:
#> [1] 6 3
#> 
#> 
#> $edges
#> $edges[[1]]
#> + 2/8 edges from f460b4d (vertex names):
#> [1] 6->3 3->6
```

### System information

```R
sessionInfo()
#> R version 4.6.0 (2026-04-24)
#> Platform: x86_64-pc-linux-gnu
#> Running under: Ubuntu 26.04 LTS
#> 
#> Matrix products: default
#> BLAS:   /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/blas/libblas.so.3.12.1 
#> LAPACK: /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/lapack/liblapack.so.3.12.1;  LAPACK version 3.12.0
#> 
#> locale:
#>  [1] LC_CTYPE=en_US.UTF-8       LC_NUMERIC=C              
#>  [3] LC_TIME=en_US.UTF-8        LC_COLLATE=en_US.UTF-8    
#>  [5] LC_MONETARY=en_US.UTF-8    LC_MESSAGES=en_US.UTF-8   
#>  [7] LC_PAPER=en_US.UTF-8       LC_NAME=C                 
#>  [9] LC_ADDRESS=C               LC_TELEPHONE=C            
#> [11] LC_MEASUREMENT=en_US.UTF-8 LC_IDENTIFICATION=C       
#> 
#> time zone: America/New_York
#> tzcode source: system (glibc)
#> 
#> attached base packages:
#> [1] stats     graphics  grDevices utils     datasets  methods   base     
#> 
#> loaded via a namespace (and not attached):
#>  [1] digest_0.6.39   fastmap_1.2.0   xfun_0.57       glue_1.8.1     
#>  [5] knitr_1.51      htmltools_0.5.9 rmarkdown_2.31  lifecycle_1.0.5
#>  [9] cli_3.6.6       reprex_2.1.1    withr_3.0.2     compiler_4.6.0 
#> [13] tools_4.6.0     evaluate_1.0.5  yaml_2.3.12     otel_0.2.0

body:

It is caused by print.igraph, on r-devel-linux-x86 64-fedora-clang only.

title: graphdb reader does not validate edge target vertex IDs
author: MarkLee131

title: Edge centrality
author: ag1805x
body:
<!-- For requests to be considered, this form must be filled out in full, and
     use cases for the feature explained in detail, ideally with references. -->

**What is the feature or improvement you would like to see?**
Currently there is just one method to calculate edge centrality in R igraph (i.e. edge betweenness). It would be good to include other edge centrality measures like k-path edge centrality or spanning edge centrality. 


**Use cases for the feature**
Converting a graph to a line graph significantly increases the number of edges making it difficult to calculate even the simple node centralities like betweenness and clustering coefficient. Having a function that directly calculates edge centralities on the original graph will be more efficient. 


**References**
[1] https://doi.org/10.1016/j.knosys.2012.01.007
[2] https://doi.org/10.1145/2736277.274112
[3] https://doi.org/10.1007/s13278-022-00876-x

title: GraphML edge ids are not imported
author: carissableker

title: Add SVD Incidence Centrality
author: verri
body:
## Summary

I would like to implement **SVD Incidence Centrality**, a unified spectral framework for computing vertex and edge centralities based on the Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) of the oriented incidence matrix. This measure is grounded in Hodge theory and provides dense rankings that avoid the sparsity issues of betweenness centrality in directed graphs.

## Motivation

SVD Incidence Centrality offers several desirable properties:

- **Unified framework**: Computes vertex and edge centralities from the same mathematical foundation, ensuring consistency
- **Direction preservation**: Naturally handles directed graphs without requiring symmetrization
- **Dense rankings**: Produces continuous centrality values for all vertices and edges
- **Spectral foundation**: Grounded in Hodge theory with connections to electrical network theory (equivalent to current-flow closeness for undirected graphs)
- **Hub/authority decomposition**: Provides principled hub and authority scores for directed networks

## Mathematical Foundation

For a graph with oriented incidence matrix $B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ ($n$ vertices, $m$ edges):

$$
B_{ve} = \begin{cases}
+w_e & \text{if edge } e \text{ enters vertex } v \\
-w_e & \text{if edge } e \text{ leaves vertex } v \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

where $w_e$ is the weight of edge $e$ (or $1$ for unweighted graphs).

The Hodge Laplacians are:

- $L_0 = BB^\top$ (vertex Laplacian, $n \times n$)
- $L_1 = B^\top B$ (edge Laplacian, $m \times m$)

Centralities are the diagonal elements of the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse:

$$
C_v(i) = [L_0^+]_{ii} \qquad C_e(e) = [L_1^+]_{ee}
$$

For directed networks, hub and authority scores aggregate edge centralities:

$$
H(i) = \alpha \cdot C_v(i) + (1-\alpha) \sum_{e \in E_{\text{out}}(i)} C_e(e)
$$

$$
A(i) = \alpha \cdot C_v(i) + (1-\alpha) \sum_{e \in E_{\text{in}}(i)} C_e(e)
$$

**Key property**: For undirected graphs, vertex SVD centrality is similar to current-flow closeness centrality (Pearson correlation $\rho \geq 0.9$ in experiments).

**Time complexity**: $O(nm\min(n, m))$ via LAPACK SVD routines.  We can use truncated SVD to achieve $O(nmk)$ using only the $k$ largest singular values/vectors.

## Proposed API

```c
igraph_error_t igraph_svd_incidence_centrality(
    const igraph_t *graph,
    igraph_vector_t *vertex_centrality,  /* NULL if not needed */
    igraph_vector_t *edge_centrality,    /* NULL if not needed */
    igraph_vector_t *hub_scores,         /* NULL if not needed */
    igraph_vector_t *authority_scores,   /* NULL if not needed */
    const igraph_vector_t *weights,
    igraph_real_t alpha
);
```

**Parameters:**

- **vertex_centrality, edge_centrality**: Normalized centrality scores $C_v(i)$ and $C_e(e)$. Pass `NULL` if not needed.
- **hub_scores, authority_scores**: Raw scores for directed networks. Pass `NULL` if not needed. Ignored for undirected graphs.
- **weights**: Optional edge weights. Pass `NULL` for unweighted graphs.
- **alpha**: Controls the balance between vertex centrality and aggregated edge centralities in hub/authority scores. With $\alpha = 0$, only edge centralities contribute; with $\alpha = 1$, only vertex centrality contributes. Ignored if both `hub_scores` and `authority_scores` are `NULL`.

## Reference

The method is described in our paper:

> Jorge Luiz Franco, Thomas Peron, Alcebiades Dal Col, Fabiano Petronetto, Filipe Alves Neto Verri, Eric K. Tokuda, Luiz Gustavo Nonato (2026). "SVD Incidence Centrality: A Unified Spectral Framework for Node and Edge Analysis in Directed Networks and Hypergraphs." arXiv preprint. [arXiv:2602.15736](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.15736)

## Questions

1. Which branch should this target? Since this adds new functionality without changing existing API, I assume `main` is appropriate.

2. Is referencing an arXiv preprint acceptable for igraph documentation, or do you prefer waiting for a peer-reviewed publication?

3. igraph's LAPACK interface currently lacks SVD wrappers. The implementation can use `igraph_lapack_dsyevr()` for eigendecomposition of the symmetric Hodge Laplacians. Is this the preferred approach, or should I also add an SVD wrapper to `igraph_lapack.h`?

## Implementation Plan

1. Add `igraph_svd_incidence_centrality()` in `src/centrality/svd_incidence.c`
2. Comprehensive tests (null graph, singleton, paths, cycles)
3. Documentation with time complexity and reference to paper
4. Entry in `interfaces/functions.yaml` for R/Python bindings

I am happy to implement this and would appreciate guidance on the above questions.

title: Add "category" field to Stimulus metadata?
author: maelle
body:
The igraph C docs are organized by category: https://igraph.org/c/html/latest/

The igraph R docs are also organized by category: https://r.igraph.org/reference/index.html

We'd like to ask whether the categories could be a YAML field in https://github.com/igraph/igraph/blob/main/interfaces/functions.yaml

So https://github.com/igraph/igraph/blob/2329c0ed9bbf5e7d41397583849d5746b36b5e29/interfaces/functions.yaml#L2629-L2633

would become

```yaml

igraph_find_cycle:
    PARAMS: |-
        GRAPH graph, OPTIONAL OUT VERTEX_INDICES vertices, OPTIONAL OUT EDGE_INDICES edges,
        NEIMODE mode=OUT
    DEPS: vertices ON graph, edges ON graph
    CATEGORY: graph-cycles
```

We'd use this information for the docs grouping but in the R case, also for splitting our `R/aaa-auto.R` script, which contains the automatically generated wrappers, into several scripts, one per category. The current script is too big, for instance I know I can't probably navigate it using symbol search in my IDE.

What do y'all think? If you agree, can we (the R igraph team) help make it happen?

title: Use evocative names instead of `graph1` and `graph2` for subisomorphism and perhaps other functions
author: krlmlr
body:
**What is the feature or improvement you would like to see?**

The `*subisomorph*` API currently uses `graph1` and `graph2` for argument names:

https://github.com/igraph/igraph/blob/2329c0ed9bbf5e7d41397583849d5746b36b5e29/interfaces/functions.yaml#L2401-L2402

Suggesting to use `haystack` and `needle`, or perhaps other descriptive names, that make it clear what the "smaller" graph is.

Are there other functions where similar confusion can arise?

**Use cases for the feature**

Make code self-explanatory.

**References**

https://github.com/igraph/rigraph/pull/2534/commits/0f2ad63053f961dea403731572ee1531296a2e6a

CC @schochastics.

title: `s_copy` has inconsistent function signatures in `s_copy.c` versus `dneupd.c`
author: LTLA
body:
**Describe the bug**

The function signature of `s_copy` in its definition at

https://github.com/igraph/igraph/blob/8771b38b375096a052223ff883bfd11580ffd6c0/vendor/f2c/s_copy.c#L17

is not consistent with the declaration at

https://github.com/igraph/igraph/blob/8771b38b375096a052223ff883bfd11580ffd6c0/vendor/lapack/dneupd.c#L340

**To reproduce**

Reproduction is a bit involved, I'm compiling to Wasm with Emscripten and the problem only shows up when linking to the **igraph** static library from another Wasm binary. I can provide a full example if necessary but hopefully the snippets above are self-evident, LMK.

Anyway, this mismatch causes linker warnings for Emscripten's linker, but I'd imagine that the same would occur for anything based off a similarly recent version of LLVM.

```
wasm-ld: warning: function signature mismatch: s_copy
>>> defined as (i64, i64, i32, i32) -> i32 in /home/luna/Code/js/scran.js/extern/installed/lib/libigraph.a(dneupd.c.o)
>>> defined as (i64, i64, i32, i32) -> void in /home/luna/Code/js/scran.js/extern/installed/lib/libigraph.a(s_copy.c.o)
```

**Version information**

**igraph** v1.0.0, obtained from the source tarball on the Releases page.

**Other comments**

FWIW Emscripten also complains about `src/graph/type_indexededgelist.c`:

```
/home/luna/Code/js/scran.js/extern/igraph/igraph-1.0.0/src/graph/type_indexededgelist.c:274:23: warning: format specifies type 'long long' but the argument has type 'long' [-Wformat]

      |         ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
  274 |                       IGRAPH_ECOUNT_MAX);
      |                       ^~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
/home/luna/Code/js/scran.js/extern/igraph/igraph-1.0.0/include/igraph_types.h:118:27: note: expanded from macro 'IGRAPH_ECOUNT_MAX'

/home/luna/Code/js/scran.js/extern/igraph/igraph-1.0.0/include/igraph_error.h:466:37: note: expanded from macro 'IGRAPH_ERRORF'

      |                       ~~~~~~
  466 |                       igraph_errno, __VA_ARGS__) ; \

/home/luna/Code/js/scran.js/extern/igraph/igraph-1.0.0/src/graph/type_indexededgelist.c:402:23: warning: format specifies type 'long long' but the argument has type 'long' [-Wformat]
  401 |         IGRAPH_ERRORF("Maximum vertex count (%" IGRAPH_PRId ") exceeded.", IGRAPH_ERANGE,

title: Misnamed test output file at `tests/unit/igraph_i_umap_fit_ab.o`
author: LTLA
body:
**Describe the bug**

Presumably the file extension is meant to be `.out`, not `.o`. Currently, this is raising some flags when I'm trying to vendor in the **igraph** sources into **Rigraphlib**, as `R CMD check` thinks it's an object file.

**To reproduce**

See https://github.com/igraph/igraph/blob/main/tests/unit/igraph_i_umap_fit_ab.o

**Version information**

File is present in the 1.0.0 tarball and also on the current `main` branch.

title: Is `igraph_setup()` safe to call repeatedly?
author: LTLA

title: examples/simple/igraph_sparsemat4.c fails on arm64, ppc64el,  riscv64, and  s390x architectures
author: jgmbenoit
body:
For igraph version 1.0.0, it appears that the example `examples/simple/igraph_sparsemat4.c` works on architectures amd64 and i386 but it  fails on architectures arm64, ppc64el,  riscv64, and  s390x architectures.
The good new is that the so called _Incorrect solution_ is the same for all these architectures and it seems very near to the _Correct solution_. So the error looks as a numerical coding error rather than an architecture issue.

You can find the _Incorrect solution_ in attachment: [igraph_1_0_0-igraph_sparsemat4-arm64+ppc64el+riscv64+s390x.res.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22992459/igraph_1_0_0-igraph_sparsemat4-arm64%2Bppc64el%2Briscv64%2Bs390x.res.txt)

title: hubs/authorities report inaccurate number of zeros
author: schochastics
body:
The functions reports the presents of more zeros than there actually are.

``` r
el <- matrix(
  c(
    1, 1, 1, 3, 3, 3, 4, 4, 5, 6, 6, 7, 7, 7, 7, 8, 8, 8, 9, 9, 10, 5, 7, 8, 10,
    8, 9, 10, 6, 9, 4, 7, 1, 6, 10, 8, 6, 7, 10, 2, 7, 1
  ),
  nrow = 21L,
  ncol = 2L
)
g <- graph_from_edgelist(el, directed = TRUE)
hs <- hits_scores(g)
#> Warning in hub_and_authority_scores_impl(graph = graph, weights = weights, : At
#> vendor/cigraph/src/centrality/hub_authority.c:81 : More than 30% of hub or
#> authority scores are zeros. The presence of zero values indicates that the
#> solution is not unique, thus the returned result may not be meaningful.
hub <- hs$hub_vector
auth <- hs$authority_vector

hub
#>  [1] 0.50132341 0.00000000 0.68439809 0.62821626 0.08653582 0.22495909
#>  [7] 1.00000000 0.82792414 0.22495909 0.12644077
auth
#>  [1] 0.35867759 0.07163074 0.00000000 0.07163074 0.15962976 0.78207620
#>  [7] 0.56651614 0.69597030 0.24547826 1.00000000
sum(hub==0)/length(hub)
#> [1] 0.1
sum(auth==0)/length(auth)
#> [1] 0.1
```

<sup>Created on 2025-10-14 with [reprex v2.1.1](https://reprex.tidyverse.org)</sup>

This happens here: https://github.com/igraph/rigraph/pull/2058 (reported here https://github.com/igraph/rigraph/issues/2202)

title: is igraph a C or C++ library ?
author: jgmbenoit
body:
The final igraph library (namely the .*so file on GNU/Linux boxes) contains C++ references, hence my question.
If it  is still a C library, is it safe to hide them ? Can you set a machinery to hide them ?

title: allow to build against an external qhull
author: jgmbenoit
body: Qhull is a well established library and my understand is that you provide a non modified version of it. Can you provide a IGRAPH_QHULL_SUPPORT `cmake`(1) conf feature similar to the ones for BLAS, LAPACK,ARPACK,GLPK,GMP,and PLFIT.

title: `igraph_rewiring_stats_t` and packed structs
author: szhorvat

title: `igraph_correlated_game()` `permutation` parameter should behave the same as `igraph_permute_vertices()`
author: szhorvat
body:
`igraph_correlated_game()` `permutation` parameter should behave the same as `igraph_permute_vertices()`

I don't know if it does. Can you check this before the 1.0 release, please, @ntamas ?

title: `igraph_sparsemat_view()` allocates memory but instructs the user not to destroy the sparsemat
author: szhorvat
body:
The design of `igraph_sparsemat_view()` seems broken.

It allocates memory but it also instructs the user not to call `igraph_sparsemat_destroy()`, but use `igraph_free()` directly on an internal member of the sparsemat `struct`.

This is ugly and problematic. If there is a clean solution at the cost of an API-break, we should include it in 1.0.

However, we've already decided to leave the sparsemat cleanup for igraph 2.0, so it might as well stay as it is ...

@ntamas, opinions? I added to the 1.0 milestone so we don't forget, but we might just decide not to fix this.

title: Should `igraph_isoclass_subgraph()` take an `igraph_vs_t`?
author: szhorvat
body:
`igraph_isoclass_subgraph()` takes a `const igraph_vector_int_t *vids` parameter. Should it takes an `igraph_vs_t vids` instead?

Arguments for `igraph_vs_t`:

 - Fits high-level interfaces more, e.g. the user can pass in vertex names instead of indices, which is much more natural. Interfaces generation is easier.

Arguments for `igraph_vector_int_t`:

 - The implementation is such as that it requires a vectors of vertex IDs anyway. In many (most) applications in C, this is exactly what we have. Converting this to an `igraph_vs_t`, then back to a vector again, will reduce performance, and is somewhat annoying.

----

It seems to me that the needs of C vs high-level languages conflict here. 

Opinions @ntamas ? I'm on the fence.
